narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30—actually above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success. The model assessed your profile, including your early academic deciles, LSAT score, family income bracket, and planned full-time enrollment, and it estimated a 51% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This close-to-even odds tipped the prediction toward failure, leading to the rejection of your application as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to pass with higher confidence. Among all the factors considered, your undergraduate GPA had the strongest positive influence, pushing the prediction toward bar exam success because it signals consistent performance beyond what most applicants show. However, your third-year school decile ranking of 2—well below the average of about 5.5—emerged as the next most influential factor, strongly pulling the prediction toward failure by highlighting relatively weaker performance in later undergrad years compared to peers. Your lower-middle family income bracket also played a notable role, nudging things slightly toward success, perhaps reflecting resilience or motivation in your background that the model associates with passing the bar. Your LSAT score of 28, notably lower than the 36.7 average, and full-time status—which is common for 93% of students—had less sway in this specific decision. Overall, while your GPA stands out favorably against typical students, the low third-year decile outweighed it, creating a risk profile that didn't align with the school's success thresholds for applicants like you. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man pursuing law, but a data-driven call based on patterns from thousands of similar profiles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering letters highlighting your family's context and determination. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted odds significantly, opening doors at schools that value your unique strengths. Many students in your position have succeeded by targeting these areas—your above-average GPA is already a great foundation to build on.
